fact: The first official cricket Test match was played in 1877 between Australia and England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground (MCG). Australia won by 45 runs, and England won the second match to draw the series.
In the 1983 Cricket World Cup, India defeated the heavily favored West Indies in the final at Lord's, London, defending a low score of 183 to win by 43 runs under captain Kapil Dev.

Sir Donald Bradman of Australia holds the highest individual batting average in Test cricket history at 99.94. He needed just 4 runs in his final innings in 1948 to achieve an average of 100, but was bowled for a duck.
In 1999, Indian leg-spinner Anil Kumble became only the second bowler in Test cricket history to take all 10 wickets in a single innings, achieving a 10-74 figure against Pakistan in Delhi.
The highest team total in a Test match innings was set by Sri Lanka in 1997 against India, scoring a massive 952/6 declared, fueled by Sanath Jayasuriya's historic 340 runs.
The first-ever Cricket World Cup was held in England in 1975, where the West Indies defeated Australia in the final by 17 runs to lift the Prudential Cup under captain Clive Lloyd.
The FIFA World Cup was first held in 1930 in Uruguay. The host nation Uruguayan team defeated Argentina 4-2 in the final match in Montevideo to become the first world champions.
Pelé of Brazil is the only player to have won three FIFA World Cup titles as an active player, winning in 1958 (Sweden), 1962 (Chile), and 1970 (Mexico).

The standard dimensions of a professional football (soccer) pitch require a length of 100-110 meters and a width of 64-75 meters for international matches. The goals must be 7.32 meters (8 yards) wide and 2.44 meters high.
In 1956, Real Madrid won the first-ever European Cup (now UEFA Champions League) by defeating Reims 4-3 in an exciting final in Paris, starting their legendary five-year reign.
Just Fontaine of France holds the unbroken record for the most goals scored in a single FIFA World Cup tournament, netting a spectacular 13 goals in the 1958 World Cup in Sweden.
In the 1966 FIFA World Cup final at Wembley, England defeated West Germany 4-2 after extra time, with Geoff Hurst scoring the only hat-trick ever recorded in a men's World Cup final.

The Thomas Cup is the premier international men's team badminton championship, established in 1948. India achieved its historic first title in 2022 by defeating traditional power Indonesia 3-0 in Bangkok.
Badminton made its official debut as a full medal sport at the Summer Olympic Games in Barcelona, Spain, in 1992, where Indonesia dominated by winning both the men's and women's singles gold medals.
The Uber Cup is the premier international women's team badminton championship, first held in 1956. China has won the tournament the most times, with over 15 titles.
In 1980, Prakash Padukone became the first Indian badminton player to win the prestigious All England Open Badminton Championships, defeating Liem Swie King in the final.
Badminton's shuttlecock consists of exactly 16 feathers plucked from the left wing of a goose or duck, designed to spin at up to 10,000 RPM when struck.

Lee Chong Wei of Malaysia holds the record for spending the most consecutive weeks as the World No. 1 badminton player, commanding the top spot for a staggering 199 consecutive weeks from 2008 to 2012.

The Wimbledon Championship, founded in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world. It is the only Grand Slam event still played on traditional grass courts, and players must adhere to a strict all-white dress code.

In 2010, John Isner of the USA and Nicolas Mahut of France played the longest match in tennis history at Wimbledon. The match lasted 11 hours and 5 minutes over three days, ending in Isner winning the final set 70-68.

Steffi Graf of Germany achieved the only 'Golden Slam' in tennis history in 1988, winning all four Grand Slam singles titles (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open) and the Olympic Gold Medal in the same calendar year.
Roger Federer was the first male player to reach 20 Grand Slam singles titles, achieving this milestone at the 2018 Australian Open by defeating Marin Cilic in five sets.
Rafael Nadal has won the French Open (Roland Garros) a record-shattering 14 times, establishing an incredible 112-4 win-loss record on the red clay of Paris.
Rod Laver of Australia is the only tennis player in the Open Era to achieve the Calendar Grand Slam twice, winning all four major titles in 1962 (as an amateur) and 1969 (as a professional).
The National Basketball Association (NBA) was founded in 1946 as the Basketball Association of America (BAA) before merging with the National Basketball League (NBL) in 1949 to form the NBA.
Dr. James Naismith invented basketball in December 1891 in Springfield, Massachusetts, using a soccer ball and two peach baskets hung on the balcony railing of a YMCA gym.
Wilt Chamberlain of the Philadelphia Warriors scored a historic 100 points in a single NBA game on March 2, 1962, against the New York Knicks, a record that has never been broken.
The Boston Celtics dominated the NBA in the 1950s and 60s, winning a record eight consecutive NBA championships from 1959 to 1966 under legendary coach Red Auerbach.

In 1992, the United States fielded the legendary 'Dream Team' at the Barcelona Olympics, featuring superstar players like Michael Jordan, Magic Johnson, and Larry Bird, winning all matches by an average of 44 points.
LeBron James became the NBA's all-time leading scorer in February 2023, surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's legendary career scoring record of 38,387 points which had stood since 1989.
Usain Bolt of Jamaica is the fastest human ever recorded, setting the 100m world record at 9.58 seconds and the 200m record at 19.19 seconds at the 2009 World Championships in Berlin.
Roger Bannister of the United Kingdom became the first person to run a mile in under four minutes on May 6, 1954, finishing in 3 minutes 59.4 seconds at Iffley Road Track in Oxford.
Florence Griffith-Joyner of the USA holds the women's 100m and 200m world records (10.49s and 21.34s, respectively), set in 1988, which remain unbroken to this day.
The marathon race is exactly 42.195 kilometers (26.2 miles) long, a distance established at the 1908 London Olympics so the race could start at Windsor Castle and finish in front of the Royal Box.
Dick Fosbury revolutionized the high jump at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics by jumping head-first with his back to the bar, a technique now known globally as the 'Fosbury Flop'.

Scuderia Ferrari is the oldest and most successful active team in Formula 1 history, having competed in every world championship since the inaugural season in 1950 and winning a record 16 Constructors' Championships.
Michael Schumacher of Germany and Lewis Hamilton of the UK are tied for the most Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships in history, with each having won 7 titles.
The Monaco Grand Prix is one of the oldest, most challenging, and prestigious races in Formula 1, held on the narrow, winding streets of Monte Carlo since 1929 with almost no run-off areas.
In 2023, Max Verstappen of Red Bull Racing set an incredible record for the most consecutive Grand Prix wins in Formula 1 history, winning 10 straight races from Miami to Monza.
Ayrton Senna of Brazil won three World Drivers' Championships (1988, 1990, 1991) with McLaren and is universally revered as one of the greatest wet-weather drivers in history.
Michael Phelps of the USA is the most decorated Olympian of all time, winning a staggering 28 Olympic medals, including a record 23 gold, 3 silver, and 2 bronze medals across five Olympic Games.
At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals in a single Games, breaking Mark Spitz's historic 1972 record of 7 gold medals in a single Olympic campaign.
An Olympic-size swimming pool is exactly 50 meters (164 feet) long, 25 meters wide, and must be at least 2 meters deep, holding approximately 2.5 million liters of water.
The butterfly stroke is the most physically demanding swimming stroke in competition, first officially recognized as a distinct style by FINA in 1952 after evolving from the breaststroke.
Katie Ledecky of the USA has dominated women's distance swimming, holding the world records for the 800m and 1500m freestyle, and winning 9 Olympic gold medals across her career.
